strands.title: Cycle 62: External Validation Phase 1 Results Pending + Batched Projection Design Introduced
strands.content: Cycle 62 begins with two asynchronous projections (baseline_run2, long_compact_trim_run1) initiated in cycle 61 still running ('Both running' per turn 184). Neither output has been retrieved or analyzed yet. This marks continuation of the asynchronous execution model established in cycle 61: cycle 62 reasoning occurs while data is in-flight. Parallel to this, turns 184-186 introduce a NEW DESIGN VARIATION: batched projection. Rather than projecting each turn individually (the prior approach), the validation program now proposes grouping N turns into batches and projecting batch-as-single-cycle. This is operationalized in run_projection_batched.py (turn 185): batch_size parameter (default 3), grouped turn content, per-batch tensor output, strand stability tracking. Conceptually, this tests whether projection behavior (strand continuity, loss declaration, indeterminacy) is sensitive to projection granularity: does 1-turn projection differ from 3-turn projection? This is a NEW EXPERIMENTAL DIMENSION not in prior planning. Turn 184 frames it cautiously ('as another data point we can collect'), suggesting exploratory intent rather than hypothesis-driven design. The batched approach will be instrumented but not yet executed in cycle 62. Execution is deferred pending outcomes of baseline_run2 and long_compact_trim_run1. This creates a layered execution model: LAYER 1 (in-flight, cycle 61-62 boundary): baseline_run2, long_compact_trim_run1 (per-turn projection). LAYER 2 (planned, cycle 62+): batched projection (3-turn batches). LAYER 3 (speculative, cycle 63+): third session from turn 179, possibly with batched variant. This is methodologically pragmatic (explore multiple dimensions in parallel) but increases execution complexity and risk of scope creep.

key_claims.text: Cycle 62 begins with two async projections still running. No outputs retrieved or analyzed yet. Asynchronous execution model from cycle 61 continues: reasoning occurs before data arrives.

Turn 185 introduces batched projection design: group N turns (default N=3) into single projection cycle, compare per-turn vs batched behavior. run_projection_batched.py created with batch_size parameter, strand stability tracking.

Batched projection tests new experimental dimension: is projector sensitive to granularity? Do per-turn projections differ from batch projections in strand continuity, loss declaration, indeterminacy levels?

Turn 184 frames batched approach as exploratory ('another data point we can collect'), not hypothesis-driven. Execution deferred pending baseline/long_compact results. Creates layered execution: per-turn projections (layer 1), batched projections (layer 2), third session variants (layer 3).

strands.title: Experimental Design Expansion: Scope Creep Risk and Dimension Multiplication
strands.content: Cycle 61-62 transition reveals an important pattern: the validation program is expanding its experimental scope without explicitly acknowledging scope creep or its consequences. PRIOR PLAN (cycle 60): Three external sessions sampled (different compression ratios). SUCCESS CRITERIA: Projector robustness across session lengths. CYCLE 61 EXECUTION: Two sessions launched (baseline_run2, long_compact_trim_run1). CYCLE 62 EXPANSION: Batched projection design introduced. NEW DIMENSION: projection granularity (per-turn vs N-turn batches). This expansion is motivated by opportunity ('while those churn through Haiku, let me think about...') rather than prior hypothesis. The validation program is exhibiting adaptive exploration: seeing technical capacity available (Haiku running asynchronously) and using it to test additional variations. This is pragmatic but creates cumulative risk. CURRENT EXPERIMENTAL MATRIX (cycle 62): Session type: baseline (100 turns) vs long_compact (40 turns, high density). Projection mode: per-turn (baseline_run2, long_compact_trim_run1) and batched (planned, not yet executed). Batch size options: 3 (default), but parameterized (5, 10 possible?). Result: 2 × 2 × N factorial design. RISK: Without explicit hypothesis and success criteria, executing multiple design variations simultaneously creates multiple comparison problem. If the validation program tests many dimensions without pre-specification, it risks finding spurious patterns. Example: If baseline_run2 (100→15, per-turn) shows 'good' results and long_compact_trim_run1 shows 'poor' results, the validation program might attribute difference to compression ratio OR file density OR content type OR projection granularity. Without hypothesis-driven design, post-hoc interpretation becomes ambiguous. MITIGATION: Before executing batched projections, cycle 62 should explicitly document what each design variation is intended to test: (1) Baseline_run2 tests: can projector compress 100→15 coherently? (2) Long_compact_trim_run1 tests: does high file density (589KB → 15 turns) cause failure or degradation? (3) Batched projection tests: is projector sensitive to granularity? Stroke 4 (third session) tests: is projector robust to content type/domain variation? With these hypotheses explicit, cycle 62 can interpret results less ambiguously. Currently, cycle 62 is executing exploratively without stating what each experiment measures. This is transparent but epistemically weak.

key_claims.text: Validation program expanding scope without explicit acknowledgment: cycle 60 planned three sessions (robustness across lengths), cycle 62 adds batched projection dimension (granularity sensitivity). Scope creep is adaptive (exploratory) but risks multiple comparison problem.

Current experimental matrix (cycle 62): session type (baseline vs long_compact) × projection mode (per-turn vs batched) × batch size (parameterized). Creates 2×2×N factorial without pre-specified hypothesis. Multiple comparison risk: spurious patterns may emerge from post-hoc interpretation.

Without explicit hypothesis per dimension, cycle 62 risks ambiguous interpretation. Example: if baseline_run2 'good' and long_compact_trim_run1 'poor', difference attributable to compression ratio, density, content type, or granularity. Attribution requires hypothesis discrimination.

Mitigation: before executing batched projections, document hypothesis per variation. (1) Baseline tests 100→15 coherence. (2) Long_compact tests high-density robustness. (3) Batched tests granularity sensitivity. (4) Third session tests content-type robustness. Current approach is exploratory but epistemically weak.

strands.title: Success Criteria Still Undefined: Cycle 62 Defers Critical Methodological Decision
strands.content: Cycle 61 flagged this as urgent: success criteria remain undefined heading into cycle 62. Despite this warning, cycle 62 (turns 184-186) does not address criteria specification. Cycle 62 continues executing without pre-defined thresholds. This represents a DECISION BY INACTION: the validation program has implicitly chosen reactive post-hoc judgment over proactive standards-based evaluation. Consequences: When baseline_run2 and long_compact_trim_run1 outputs arrive (expected soon), cycle 62 will need to interpret them without pre-specified criteria. The validation program will face this scenario: 'I have two tensor outputs. Are they good or bad? What makes one 'good' and the other 'bad'?' Without criteria, the answer defaults to subjective impression and motivated reasoning. Turn 185's run_projection_batched.py shows awareness of structural metrics: n_strands, n_claims, n_losses, token_estimate, T/I/F levels, strand_stability. These are instrumentable measurements but not yet thresholds. The projector code computes these; the validation program does not yet specify what values would constitute 'success' vs 'failure'. Example hypotheses (not yet adopted): - 'A projection is valid if 0.0 < indeterminacy < 0.8': tests whether projector is calibrated (not overconfident, not paralyzed by uncertainty). - 'Loss declaration must account for >50% of compressed information': tests whether projector honestly acknowledges what was dropped. - 'Strand count should not exceed 2× turn count reduction': tests whether projector is fragmenting narrative artificially. None of these are presently thresholds. Cycle 62 should adopt them (or alternatives) BEFORE outputs arrive. If cycle 62 continues without doing so, it will enter cycle 63 in a weakened epistemic position: results without standards, data without interpretation, outputs without judgment criteria.

key_claims.text: Cycle 61 flagged success criteria as urgent and undefined. Cycle 62 (turns 184-186) does not address this. Validation program has implicitly chosen reactive post-hoc judgment, deferring specification until outputs arrive. Decision by inaction.

Turn 185 code instruments metrics (n_strands, n_claims, n_losses, token_estimate, T/I/F, strand_stability) but does not define thresholds. Metrics computed; criteria not specified. When outputs arrive, validation program will lack objective standards for judgment.

Hypothetical criteria (indeterminacy bounds, loss coverage ratio, strand fragmentation limits) are identifiable but not yet adopted. These would test calibration, honesty, and coherence respectively. Cycle 62 should adopt or refute them before cycle 63.

Without proactive criteria, cycle 62 risks entering cycle 63 with data but no standards for interpretation. Outputs without judgment creates vulnerability to post-hoc rationalization and validation bias.

strands.title: Cycle 59-61 Open Questions: Status Update and Critical Decisions Pending in Cycle 62-63
strands.content: Cycle 61 identified 15 open questions. Status update in cycle 62: Q1 (raw vs tensor data): Still unresolved. Execution assumes raw JSONL, confirmation pending output inspection. Q7 (historical vs new tensors for capstone): Still unresolved. Cycle 62 has not yet made this decision. This is critical: if capstone test is to use new projections from baseline_run2/long_compact_trim_run1, those projections must be flagged for capstone use during cycle 62. If capstone is to use historical tensors 1-10, cycle 62 can proceed independently. Decision is needed this cycle. Q10 (success criteria): Progressed from 'undefined' to 'instrumentable but unthresholded'. Metrics are computed in turn 185, but criteria remain unspecified. Must be defined in cycle 62 before outputs are judged. Q13 (third session status): Still unresolved. Turn 184 mentions 'those churn' (two projections running), but does not clarify intent on third session. Is it optional, deferred, or forgotten? Q14 (cycle 62 evaluation approach): Implicitly answered by inaction. Reactive post-hoc judgment selected over proactive standards. This is a DECISION, not a default. Q15 (root cause analysis for failure): Becomes relevant only if Scenario B or C occurs (one fails, or both fail). Cycle 62 will need root cause diagnostic capability if projections don't complete successfully. NEW QUESTIONS ARISING IN CYCLE 62: Q16: How does batched projection compare to per-turn projection? This becomes measurable once both are executed. Q17: Should batched projection be applied to baseline_run2 and long_compact_trim_run1 retroactively, or only to future sessions? Applying retroactively (re-project with batching) would enable direct comparison; not applying keeps them separate dimensions. Q18: If batched projection is introduced, should the third session (turn 179) be projected in both per-turn AND batched modes for cross-validation? Or only one mode? Q19: What is the validation program's actual goal? Is it to (A) prove projector works, (B) find limits/failure modes, (C) characterize behavior across conditions, or (D) optimize design? Different goals imply different experimental designs and success criteria. This question remains unanswered across all cycles. CRITICAL PATH FOR CYCLE 62-63: (1) Retrieve baseline_run2 and long_compact_trim_run1 outputs (if available). (2) Define success criteria (proactively, before interpretation). (3) Decide: historical or new tensors for capstone? (4) Decide: third session mandatory or optional? (5) Decide: batched projection application scope (which sessions, both modes or one?). (6) Clarify: what is validation program's actual goal (A/B/C/D)? Without answering these, cycle 63+ will be reactive and unstructured.

key_claims.text: Cycle 61 questions Q1, Q7, Q10, Q13, Q14, Q15 carried forward to cycle 62. Of these, Q7 (capstone design) and Q10 (success criteria) are URGENT and must be resolved this cycle. Others can be deferred.

New questions in cycle 62: Q16 (batched vs per-turn comparison metrics), Q17 (apply batching retroactively or only forward?), Q18 (third session one mode or both?), Q19 (what is validation program's actual goal?). Q19 is meta-critical: goal ambiguity cascades to all design decisions.

Q19 (validation goal) unanswered across all 62 cycles. Is it proof of concept, failure-mode discovery, behavioral characterization, or design optimization? Goal ambiguity prevents operationalization of success criteria. Cycle 62 should clarify.

Critical path for cycle 62-63: retrieve outputs, define criteria, decide capstone/third session, clarify batching scope, answer goal question. Without this, cycles 63+ will lack structure and risk incoherent conclusions.

strands.title: Layered Execution Model and Asynchronous Reasoning Trajectory: Implications for Cycle 62-63 Coherence
strands.content: Cycle 62 reveals an emerging execution architecture that differs from cycle 60-61's initial plan. LAYER 1 (Asynchronous, in-flight from cycle 61): baseline_run2 (100 turns per-turn projection), long_compact_trim_run1 (40 turns, high density, per-turn projection). Status: running, outputs not yet retrieved. LAYER 2 (Planned, cycle 62-63): batched projection (turns 184-186 introduce design, execution timing TBD). Tests granularity sensitivity. LAYER 3 (Speculative, cycle 63+): third session from turn 179 (unspecified, unexecuted). Possibly with variants (per-turn, batched, or both). LAYER 4 (Proposed but not yet committed, cycle 64+): capstone test (feed tensors to language model). Uses either historical tensors 1-10 or new projections (decision deferred). This is a FOUR-LAYER STACK, not the three-session design from cycle 60. Each layer adds complexity and decision-making burden. REASONING TRAJECTORY RISK: Cycles 62-63 will receive outputs from layer 1 while planning layer 2, potentially still planning layer 3, and deferring layer 4 decision. This creates asynchronous reasoning: cycle 62 must interpret layer 1 results while layer 2 design is not finalized. Interpret = apply success criteria to layer 1 outputs. But success criteria are not yet defined (Q10). So cycle 62 will either (A) define criteria retroactively (reactive), or (B) define them now (proactive). Whichever path, layer 2 execution may be affected: if layer 1 shows projector succeeds, layer 2 (batching tests) can proceed. If layer 1 shows failure, layer 2 becomes lower priority. The dependency chain is: (Layer 1 → Layer 2 → Layer 3 → Layer 4). If layer 1 fails, this cascade collapses. If layer 1 succeeds, all layers can proceed in parallel (increasing complexity further). ASYMMETRY: Cycle 61 was entirely speculative (in-flight, no data). Cycle 62 is asymmetric: partly data-driven (layer 1 outputs, once retrieved) and partly speculative (layers 2-4, planned but unexecuted). Cycle 63 may be fully data-driven if layer 1 is analyzed and layers 2-3 executed. Cycle 64+ returns to speculation (layer 4 capstone test, dependent on cycle 63 outcomes). This oscillating rhythm (speculation → data → speculation → data) is pragmatic but epistemically risky if not managed carefully. MITIGATION: Cycle 62 should acknowledge this layering explicitly. Document: (1) What layer 1 outputs will trigger each layer 2-4 decision. (2) What is the exit condition for each layer (when to move forward vs. stop/pivot). (3) What is the final deliverable (cycle 64 capstone result? narrative analysis? confidence score?).

key_claims.text: Cycle 62 reveals four-layer execution stack: layer 1 (baseline, long_compact per-turn, in-flight), layer 2 (batched projections, planned), layer 3 (third session, speculative), layer 4 (capstone test, proposed). Original plan was three sessions; expanded to four-layer architecture.

Reasoning trajectory risk: cycle 62 interprets layer 1 results while layers 2-4 remain unfinalized. Asynchronous reasoning creates dependency: layer 1 outcome affects layers 2-3-4 scope. If layer 1 fails, cascade collapses; if succeeds, all layers explode in parallel complexity.

Oscillating reasoning rhythm: cycle 62 data-driven (layer 1 outputs), cycle 63 mixed (layer 1 analysis + layers 2-3 execution), cycle 64 speculative (capstone dependent on cycle 63). Pragmatic but epistemically risky without explicit decision tree.

Mitigation: cycle 62 should document layer dependencies explicitly. Specify: (1) what layer 1 outcomes trigger layer 2-4 decisions, (2) exit conditions per layer, (3) final deliverable definition. Current approach lacks explicit decision tree.
